data.title: Global central banks face war-driven inflation test as bond markets brace for signals
data.description:
Investors are heading into a pivotal week for global monetary policy, with the world’s leading central banks set to deliver interest-rate decisions against the backdrop of rising geopolitical tensions and renewed inflation concerns, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

The Federal Reserve, European Central Bank, Bank of Japan, Bank of England and Bank of Canada are all scheduled to meet, creating a rare moment when every Group of Seven central bank is simultaneously weighing policy for economies that collectively represent roughly half of global output. Markets broadly expect officials to leave rates unchanged, but attention will focus on how policymakers describe inflation risks tied to the U.S.-Iran conflict and higher energy prices.

Any indication that central bankers are prepared to maintain restrictive policy for longer, or even tighten further, could pressure government bonds. Sovereign debt has lagged equities and credit markets in recent weeks as investors largely looked through the conflict and continued buying risk assets.

Amy Xie Patrick of Pendal Group said policymakers have little downside in sounding firm on inflation now, particularly given the uncertain outlook for oil prices and broader price pressures. She said she has removed duration exposure from her portfolio this month.

Bond markets have remained unusually calm despite the geopolitical backdrop. Yields on short-dated notes in major markets have stayed relatively elevated, while day-to-day moves in one- to three-year maturities have been smaller than in March. Some investors believe that calm could end if policymakers adopt a more forceful tone this week.

Stephen Miller, formerly of BlackRock in Australia and now a consultant at GSFM, said central banks remain wary of underestimating inflation after the policy mistakes made during the pandemic, when many officials initially described price pressures as temporary.

In the United Kingdom, Bank of England officials have already warned that the conflict could worsen inflation. U.K. consumer prices rose 3.3% in March from a year earlier, up from 3.0% in February, helped by higher fuel costs. Markets responded last week by shifting from pricing one rate increase this year to expecting at least two.

In the United States, Federal Reserve officials have acknowledged that sustained higher oil prices could complicate the inflation outlook and potentially affect the rate path. At the same time, resilient employment and retail sales data suggest the economy has not yet materially weakened.

TD Securities strategist Molly Brooks expects Fed Chair Jerome Powell to emphasize policy neutrality while acknowledging that recent energy-driven inflation pressures have added uncertainty. She said the firm expects the 10-year Treasury yield to remain in a roughly 4.1% to 4.4% range absent clearer guidance.

Elsewhere, Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda has stressed the need to weigh both upside and downside inflation risks, while analysts at Evercore ISI expect the BOJ to hold rates steady but maintain a hawkish bias. ECB President Christine Lagarde is also expected to reiterate that uncertainty remains high, even as markets continue to anticipate additional tightening in coming months.

Still, the inflation story is only part of the equation. If higher energy prices and geopolitical stress begin to undermine demand, central banks may eventually need to pivot toward growth concerns. That could ultimately pull yields lower after the current focus on inflation fades.

data.title: Asia’s demographic squeeze may not be destiny, BofA economists say
data.description:
Asia’s shrinking birth rates and rapidly aging populations are poised to reshape the region’s economic outlook, but the slowdown is not inevitable if governments and businesses successfully deploy artificial intelligence, invest in education and encourage longer working lives, according to a new report from Bank of America Securities economists Yvonne He and Helen Qiao.

Fertility falls below replacement levels

The April 26 report said Asia’s average total fertility rate has fallen from roughly 6.0 births per woman in the 1960s to an estimated 1.87 in 2025, below the replacement rate of 2.1. Japan and South Korea are aging fastest, followed by China and Thailand, while India and Indonesia still retain younger populations and a longer demographic growth runway.

The economists said the drop in fertility reflects rising child-rearing costs, urbanization, expensive housing and education, as well as greater career opportunities for women that have increased the opportunity cost of childbearing.

Growth headwinds build

Population aging has long been linked to weaker economic growth, softer productivity and disinflationary pressure. BofA cited International Monetary Fund estimates that demographic headwinds alone could trim global GDP growth by about 1.1 percentage points over 2025 to 2050 versus 2016 to 2018 trends.

A shrinking working-age population limits labor supply, while older societies may generate fewer new ideas and less entrepreneurial dynamism, the report said. At the same time, slower household formation and weaker consumption demand can weigh on inflation.

Three main offsets

BofA argued that aging does not have to equal stagnation. The report identified three policy levers that could partly offset demographic drag: stronger human capital, faster AI adoption and delayed retirement.

The economists said healthier and better-educated workers can sustain output even as workforce numbers decline. They also pointed to rising AI use globally, citing surveys showing 88% of organizations now regularly use AI in at least one business function, up from 78% a year earlier.

Longer careers could also help. Many Asian countries still have relatively low effective retirement ages compared with OECD averages, leaving room for gradual increases. China and Vietnam have already begun raising statutory retirement ages.

Different countries, different paths

The report emphasized that Asia is not a single demographic story.

Japan, Korea and Singapore are already at the frontier of aging and are leaning harder into automation and productivity upgrades. China and Thailand are entering a more compressed transition phase where policy reforms could still soften the blow. India and Indonesia, by contrast, remain younger and should focus on education, infrastructure and formal job creation rather than aging pressures for now.

Winners in the ‘Silver Economy’

As societies age, spending patterns are shifting. BofA expects stronger demand for wellness, fitness, preventive healthcare, travel, leisure and services tailored to older consumers. Smaller households may also lift demand for convenience foods, ready-to-eat meals and delivery services.

That means the demographic story may not just be about fewer babies. It may also be about more robots, older workers and consumers spending more on health, experiences and staying active.

data.title: Musk nears launch of X Money as investors watch ‘everything app’ bet
data.description:
More than three years after buying Twitter, Elon Musk is preparing to roll out X Money, a payments and banking service inside X that would advance his goal of turning the platform into an “everything app.” Early access users say a public launch could come soon, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

Though X is privately held, the initiative could affect other publicly traded companies in opening new revenue streams for X through payments, banking products and commerce while strengthening user engagement. It is also a test of whether Musk can build a U.S. version of Asia’s super-app model, with implications for fintech rivals, advertising strategy and the value of Musk’s broader business ecosystem.

Users testing the service have described features including free peer-to-peer transfers, a Visa (V) debit card, 3% cash back on some purchases and a 6% yield on savings balances. Reports also suggest an AI assistant from Musk’s xAI venture could help users track spending and manage transactions.

Musk has long said he wants X to become a platform where users can handle daily life, from payments to shopping and financial services. The model resembles China’s WeChat, the app from Tencent Holdings (TCEHY) (TCTZF) which combines messaging, commerce and banking in one app.

Still, challenges remain. X is said to lack money-transmitter licenses in several states, including New York and Massachusetts, limiting nationwide expansion. Regulators have also raised questions about security controls, business practices and consumer safeguards.

Analysts say peer-to-peer payments alone may not be enough. The bigger opportunity would come if X can persuade users to make it their primary financial account, opening the door to deposits, lending and broader commerce activity.

Skeptics note Musk has repeatedly missed earlier launch targets and still needs smoother in-app shopping tools to make commerce seamless. But if X Money gains traction, it could become one of the boldest attempts yet to merge social media and finance in the U.S.

data.title: What would the S&P looks like with SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic?
data.description:
Here's a hypothetical: What would the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPY) (IVV) (VOO) look like if three of the most anticipated IPOs of the year dropped out of the sky today and into the benchmark index.

Mega IPOs from SpaceX (SPACE), OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) could significantly reshape the structure of the S&P, increasing concentration risk and reinforcing the market’s reliance on AI-driven themes, in a scenario based on current valuations.

As of late March, the S&P 500’s top 10 holdings — led by Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), and Microsoft (MSFT) — accounted for roughly 36.5% of the index, with a total market cap of about $57.6T.

In a scenario where SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic were added at valuations of roughly $1.75T (including Starlink profitability potential), $1T, and $380B, respectively, the index would absorb about $3.13T in net new market cap after removing underperforming tech names such as Salesforce (CRM), Workday (WDAY), and Intuit (INTU), along with The Trade Desk (TTD).

 

 

Index implications

The additions would push the S&P 500’s total value to approximately $60.3T. SpaceX alone would rank would barge its way into the Magnificent 7 or Great 8 or whatever is next, while the combined weight of the three new entrants would reach about 5.2%.

That shift would lift top-10 concentration back above 40%, reintroducing a level of crowding that analysts have warned about — but with a key difference: today’s largest companies are increasingly tied to a shared AI infrastructure narrative.

The scenario also highlights the scale challenge. With typical public floats of 15% to 25%, these IPOs would require $432B to $576B of capital absorption, far exceeding total U.S. IPO proceeds over the past decade.

S&P 500 inclusion rules — including profitability requirements and a seasoning period — mean such changes would not occur immediately.

data.title: Art market slumps as private jets and yachts leave it behind
data.description:
The global art market is showing unusual weakness, with sales stagnating even as other industries serving the ultrawealthy continue to thrive, according to The Wall Street Journal’s marquee “Heard on the Street” column. Private jet deliveries reached a 15-year high in 2025, while superyacht sales hit a record, highlighting the contrast with sluggish demand for fine art.

Global art sales rose 4% in 2025, according to the Art Basel and UBS Art Market Report, but remained well below the highs of 2022 and still trailed 2019 levels by 7%. The soft performance is especially notable in the U.S., where stock markets remain near record highs and wealthy households have benefited from rising portfolios.

The market now faces a key test during New York’s spring auctions next month. Top-quality works are expected to attract strong bidding, but demand for lesser pieces may remain uneven. Auction watchers will closely track how many lots carry guaranteed minimum bids, a common sign that sellers want protection in uncertain markets.

One factor weighing on demand may be the shift toward viewing art as an investment asset. In recent years, hedge fund managers and private equity executives often used art collections as collateral for low-cost loans, then invested borrowed cash elsewhere. Higher interest rates have made that strategy less attractive and increased the cost of holding expensive works.

Art has also disappointed many short-term speculators. Data from Bank of America and ArtTactic showed works sold in the U.S. in 2025 that had been purchased within the prior five years lost an average of 5.7% annually. Owners typically need to hold art for at least a decade to break even, before accounting for insurance, commissions and taxes.

Another challenge may be changing tastes. Baby boomers helped drive huge prices for postwar and contemporary artists such as Mark Rothko, Andy Warhol and Francis Bacon. Younger collectors are increasingly drawn to works by Black artists and women artists that were previously overlooked.

That generational shift could pressure values for established names. The value of Warhol works sold at auction last year was 85% below 2022 levels, according to ArtTactic. Whether that proves temporary or signals a broader change remains unclear.

Many longtime collectors may welcome the retreat of speculators who inflated prices in recent years. But their exit also underscores the difficulty the art market faces in generating sustained growth.

data.title: Gunman’s manifesto raises security questions, but traders may focus on risk response
data.description:
Authorities are reviewing a manifesto allegedly sent by White House Correspondents’ Dinner shooting suspect Cole Allen shortly before Saturday’s attack, a document centered on political grievances and security failures rather than specific corporate or industry targets.

The incident may afffect investors because sudden political violence can trigger short-term risk-off trading, boost demand for safe-haven assets and lift shares tied to defense, cybersecurity or security infrastructure. Trading strategies often shift quickly toward volatility hedges, Treasury bonds, gold or companies seen as beneficiaries of heightened security spending.

According to the document obtained by the New York Post, Allen said he intended to target administration officials and described law-enforcement personnel as secondary targets only if necessary. He repeatedly criticized event security, writing that he encountered “No damn security” during travel, at the hotel and inside the event.

The manifesto didn’t identify corporations, publicly traded companies or specific business sectors as intended targets. However, it referenced technology broadly, noting the suspect worked in the field and criticizing security procedures. It also mentioned Iran in a hypothetical comparison involving foreign agents, but not energy firms or industries directly.

The case differs sharply from the alleged killing of a UnitedHealthcare (UNH) Chief Executive Brian Thompson in 2024, where the victim was tied directly to a major publicly traded healthcare company and the event immediately raised questions about executive security, healthcare-sector backlash and corporate reputational risk. In the White House dinner case, the stated targets were political figures rather than business leaders, making the market implications more focused on government security spending and headline-driven volatility than on a specific company or industry.

Markets often separate isolated domestic security incidents from broader economic fundamentals unless they point to sustained instability or policy change. Still, such events can briefly raise volatility, particularly in defense names, insurers, travel operators and companies linked to event security.

For now, investors are likely to watch whether the episode leads to increased federal security spending, venue-security upgrades or broader political fallout rather than any direct corporate implications.

data.title: Supreme Court Roundup case could reshape Bayer’s legal risk
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court hears arguments Monday in Bayer’s (BAYZF) (BAYRY) bid to curb thousands of lawsuits claiming its Roundup weedkiller causes cancer, a closely watched case in the company’s long-running legal battle over the product. A ruling is expected by the end of June, Reuters reported.

The outcome is important to investors because it could significantly affect Bayer’s (BAYZF) (BAYRY) future litigation costs, settlement exposure and balance-sheet uncertainty. A victory could reduce one of the biggest overhangs on the stock, while a loss may leave the company facing years of continued legal payouts.

The case stems from a Missouri lawsuit brought by John Durnell, who said he developed non-Hodgkin lymphoma after years of Roundup exposure. A jury awarded him $1.25 million, and a state appeals court upheld the verdict.

At issue is whether federal pesticide-labeling law overrides state failure-to-warn claims. Bayer argues that because the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency has found no cancer risk from glyphosate and does not require a warning label, state-law claims should be blocked. The Trump administration has backed Bayer’s position.

Bayer (BAYZF) (BAYRY) still faces roughly 65,000 claims in U.S. courts. Plaintiffs allege Roundup caused cancer, while Bayer (BAYZF) (BAYRY) maintains decades of research show the product and its active ingredient are safe when used properly.

Earlier this year, Bayer (BAYZF) (BAYRY) agreed to a $7.25 billion proposed settlement intended to resolve many current and future claims. Final court approval is still pending, with a hearing scheduled for July.

Even if Bayer (BAYZF) (BAYRY) wins at the Supreme Court, the litigation would not automatically end. Many lawsuits also include allegations of negligence, misleading marketing and product defects that could continue independently of the labeling issue.

Still, a favorable ruling could strengthen Bayer’s (BAYZF) (BAYRY) hand in appeals and limit claims brought by plaintiffs who reject the settlement. Those opting out must decide by June 4, potentially before the Supreme Court issues its decision.

data.title: Supreme Court to hear geofence warrant case Monday in key privacy test
data.description:
The U.S. Supreme Court will hear arguments Monday in a closely watched case over so-called geofence warrants, a police tool used to obtain cellphone-location data from technology companies to identify people near crime scenes. A ruling is expected by early July.

The case matters to investors because it could reshape privacy rules for major technology companies, affecting data collection practices, legal exposure and advertising models. It may also influence trading strategies in tech stocks, as tighter limits on user data could pressure valuations for firms reliant on location-based services or digital targeting.

The dispute centers on whether broad location-based warrants violate the Fourth Amendment’s protections against unreasonable searches. Police use geofence warrants to request data on devices that were within a specific area during a set period. Critics say the practice can sweep in innocent people and expose sensitive personal movements.

The case was brought by Okello Chatrie, who is serving a 12-year sentence for robbing a Virginia credit union. He argues the geofence warrant used to identify him was unconstitutional.

Supporters of the practice say it is an important investigative tool. The Trump administration argued in court filings that eliminating geofence warrants would “handicap the investigation of major crimes.”

Privacy groups and technology companies have lined up against the warrants. Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) told the court that location history can amount to a “digital diary of a person’s travels.”

Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) has already reduced its exposure by moving much user location history from company servers to individual devices, limiting its ability to respond to broad location searches. Still, the ruling could affect how law enforcement seeks user data from many technology platforms.

For markets, the decision may be another test of whether privacy regulation becomes a larger headwind for data-driven tech businesses.

data.title: Tillis says Powell may stay as Fed governor during 'lengthy' DOJ appeal
data.description:
While Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) indicated the process to confirm Kevin Warsh as the new Federal Reserve chairman will go forward, he also raised a scenario where current chief Jay Powell stays on the Board of Governors.

Speaking on NBC’s "Meet the Press," Tillis said the Department of Justice will proceed with an appeal of Judge James Boasberg’s ruling to quash subpoenas tied to the Fed, but emphasized that the appeal is limited in scope.

“That appeal is simply to challenge the basis for the ruling on the motion to quash the subpoenas,” Tillis said.

“Mr. Powell will have to make his own decisions at some point in the future about when and if he leaves the board before the end of his ‘28 term expiration," Tillis added. "I suspect Mr. Powell wants to see what happens with the appeal and to make sure that it is fully settled after the appeal. It could be a lengthy process. Hopefully not. And then we can move on and let the Fed be independent.”

Prediction markets currently show odds of more than 80% that Powell leaves the Fed before the end of this year.

data.title: Global equities end the week mixed as energy risks, earnings dominate sentiment
data.description:
Markets started the week strong on tech earnings and Wall Street gains but turned cautious as Middle East tensions escalated. Disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz tightened energy supply, lifted rate expectations, and weighed on equities, while a ceasefire extension between Israel and Lebanon offered only limited relief. 

Out of the 91 S&P 500 companies that reported earnings this week, 75 of them beat EPS estimates, while 66 of those names surpassed revenue expectations.

For the week, the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq (COMP:IND) rose 0.6% and 1.7%, while Dow (DJI) booked a 0.5% fall.

Across the Atlantic, the European equities (STOXX) ended the week 1.6% lower as stalled US-Iran talks and a closed Strait of Hormuz dimmed hopes of a quick resolution.

Data released during the session showed higher energy prices pushed Eurozone economic activity into contraction.

UK retail sales rose in March, supported by strong fuel demand. At the same time, weaker business confidence and rising cost pressures were signalled by PMI data, while inflation picked up amid higher energy prices linked to the Iran conflict.

In the week, London’s FTSE 100 (UKX) equities declined 2.7% while Germany (DAX:IND) and France (CAC:IND) markets slipped 1.1% and 2.1%, respectively.

The Asia-Pacific markets 

China’s central bank kept its key lending rates unchanged for another month, in line with expectations, maintaining them at record-low levels.

In the last week, Chinese markets (SHCOMP) added 0.7%, while Japan’s Nikkei 225 (NKY:IND) rose 1.5%.

Japan’s manufacturing activity strengthened in April, beating expectations. Inflation also edged higher at the fastest pace in over a year in March, driven partly by rising transport costs linked to Middle East tensions.

ETFs to track Europe: ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

ETFs to track Asia & Oceania: (NYSEARCA:EWJ), (NYSE:JEQ), (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:DXJ), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:FXY), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSEARCA:KWEB)
